sentences.text: Yeah, coming back to Nonantola.
So we lived there, and I started working for-- for the farm-- for the farmers.
You know, and we [? start ?] to get friendly with the village and everything else.
And we got to know each other.
And 1943, Italy declares armistice.
So far, the-- [INAUDIBLE] the-- Mussolini was arrested, and what happens?
The Nazis, the German army, occupies northern Italy.
Naturally, includes Nonantola.
Here we are again.
[LAUGHS] The German-- German army, and after awhile, they start looking-- they start looking for Jews.
An entire village told them, there's no Jews in this village.
Everybody.
Nobody betrayed us.
Even though there were fascist functionaries [? people, ?] Nobody.
Later on, some of us were going in hiding with farmers, and some-- some of the kids were hiding in-- there's a very famous monastery there called San Sylvestro, S-A-N S-Y-L-V-E-S-T-R-O.
San Sylvestro is the patron saint, and some of the-- some of the children later on put into this-- into this monastery, hiding.
And later on, Don Beccari, Dr.
Morealli were able to secure phony papers and somehow get the kids out.
But I wanna-- I wanna come back to an episode that happened.
I was there.
This-- this-- this other kid, his aunt lived up in Asti, in northern Italy, near Turin.
And he wanted to go and see his aunt.
I was a very adventurous guy, not-- not like today.
And I said, I'll go with you.
You didn't need anything because everything was total chaos.
You know, you hop on the train and just go.
Nobody asked you for anything.
No-- no tickets, nothing.
We went to Asti, and on the way back, the-- the Germans had imposed a curfew.
So you had to be-- by 10:00-- the-- the train going back to an Nonantola, it did not leave until 11 o'clock.
So by 10 o'clock, everybody had to be off the streets.
You could be on the train, but not on the street.
So this fella and I-- his name is Robert [?
Weiss. ?] He lives somewhere in Florida.
We've lost-- we've lost contact.
We sat on the train and waited for the train to leave a 11 o'clock.
In this train compartment, there were other Italians sitting there.
And they-- they were cursing Mussolini and Hitler and all.
They were Italians.
Couldn't-- couldn't care less.
So Bobby [?
Weiss ?] and I started talking.
Even though we both talk Italian, you know, we talk German among ourselves.
So all of a sudden, you could hear these Italians.
All of a sudden, they became quiet.
They shut up like a clam.
And I said to Bobby, you know, I think these guys think we're-- we're Germans.
And you know Italians.
They could take us and toss out of the-- toss us right out of the train.
So we told them.
Gentlemen, we are not Germans.
We are Jews.
We are trying to save ourselves.
They say, Jews?
What are you guys doing here?
Well, I say, we live in Nonantola.
We don't know where to go.
He said, wait a minute.
Wait a minute.
I-- I live up at the Swiss border.
I can get you-- get you guys into Switzerland.
Into Switzerland?
I said, there must be a German soldier every 10 yards.
He said, no, no, no, no.
There's nobody there.
I know a way.
I can get you-- I can get you into Switzerland.
You know, I smuggle you at night, and I know a place where you can go.
Here's my name and my telephone number.
If you need me, call me.
Get in touch with me, and I'll see what I can do.
Well, you know, you don't-- something like this, you don't-- you don't just don't take it for face value.
You know, you didn't trust.
So we went back to Nonantola and just talked it over with [?
Yosco ?] [?
Yusef, ?] our [INAUDIBLE], and everybody else.
He says, look fellas.
You're on your own.
If you want to take the chance and do this, go ahead with my blessing.
You know.
You're old enough, you're big enough, you can handle-- you're not little kids or girls.
You can handle it if you want to do it.
So we decided to go for it.
You know.
So a couple of other people joined us, too.
And we went back up there to that guy.
And [? he said-- ?] he put us up in his house.
He lived in Italy, on the Italian side.
And he said, I'm going to get you to another place.
I think tomorrow or the next day, it's gonna start raining.
And the Germans are gonna be drunk somewhere.
You know.
If it's raining, they don't like to be out doing guard duty.
I know a place where you can go right through the barbed wire and reach-- reach Switzerland.
Once you're over there, you know, see if you can make it to-- to the next-- the next railroad station, and go.
And then good luck to you.
So we did.
As a matter of fact, we were joined-- we were joined by an Italian priest, a captain of the Italian army.
We also learned how to say Catholic prayers, you know, the Ava Maria, and the Padre Nuestro, and all this kind of stuff in case a question us.
We're not Jewish, we're Italians.
So we made it.
We went to-- we went to his place.
He then took us to another place, closer to the border.
We stayed there two nights.
And the next night, it started raining cats and dogs.
And we-- we took off through the barbed wire into Switzerland.
When we reached the railroad station, there was a bunch of Swiss students who had just been on a-- on a-- on a camping trip or whatever it was.
So I had enough Swiss money with me for me to buy a German Swiss newspaper, which I could read anyway.
So we just mixed in with these kids.
We were just sitting there.
I took the-- I took the German paper, just sat there, and read-- started reading the paper.
So the train took off.
We-- We had money to buy a ticket, OK?
So I had money to buy.
I bought a ticket to Zurich.
And the-- I guess the Swiss police came by, and he just looked.
Yeah, a bunch of young, Swiss students.
I made it all the way to Zurich from the Italian border.
Then I contacted [INAUDIBLE].
There was some-- a couple other people.
We contacted-- once we reached Zurich, at that time, I had an uncle living in Zurich who was still alive.
We contacted the Jewish agency.
His name was Nathan Schwalb, S-C-H-W-A-L-B.
He was the head of the Jewish agency, or the youth Aliyah in-- in Zurich.
And we told him about the other-- other children still back in Italy.
And told him to get the wheels in motion.
Try to get them over, which he did.
And later on, the-- the children-- we were all able to bring them to-- to-- a few of them ventured down to the-- to the Allied lines.
Some older people.
They went down south and crossed-- crossed the Allied lines and made it.
Thank God, everybody made it.
So the children made it to Switzerland.
All this is documented in a film made years ago by Israel television.
It's called The children of the Villa Emma.
This whole thing was, you know, was narrated.
As a matter of fact, some people, some real people participated.
People that lived in [INAUDIBLE], Italians.
Don Arrigo Beccari is one of them.
And then participants of this thing are there.
It's-- it's all in Hebrew with English subtitles.
I have the copies-- I have a copy of that.
So after awhile, I was about 10 days in Zurich and made all the contacts and everything else.
And I was told, you know, you can't hang around here.
This is-- this is not Italy.
Told me, ?] there's no confusion here.
Everything is very well-organized.
You better go to this-- to-- you've gotta go-- go to the police and present yourself.
The only thing is, don't tell them you've been here for 10 days.
Tell them you got here yesterday, and we told you immediately to present yourself to the Swiss police.
That's what I did.
So after interrogating me, they put me in jail.
They couldn't believe it.
They said, no.
How could you?
It's not-- it's impossible to get all the way from-- from-- from Chiasso, from Lugano, up here to Zurich without being detected.
I said, I just sat there.
Nobody-- nobody asked me for anything.
After two days, you know, they start to call my uncle and the Jewish agency.
They told them who I was.
And they-- they put-- they put me into a refugee camp.
I was in several refugee camps.
You know.
The biggest thing was boredom.
You really didn't know what to do.
You could-- but we were under guard.
You couldn't leave the camp.
Refugee camp, I mean, where they were?
In Switzerland, all over the place.
In Alton, near Basel.
I was in several ones.
I was in Alton.
I was in Zofingen, all different places.
You know, I forgot already.

Then the-- later on, I was able-- I became an interpreter for the Swiss military police, because being fluent in Italian, being fluent in German, and, you know, I had a pretty good knowledge of English, because in Germany, I learned English.
I studied English.
Not like today, but I could get by.
So I became an interpreter.
And I was interrogating Germans, German soldiers that escaped into Switzerland.
You know.
And the odd part about it was nobody was a Nazi.
They didn't like Hitler.
That's what they told me.
And I told them point blank, don't-- don't give me that business.
I was one of you.
I grew up with you.
You loved Hitler.
And everything else.
You know, anyway.
But I interrogated them.
So later on, I was able to get-- to get a scholarship.
Well, then I asked to be transferred to Zurich.
Zurich, they had a-- they had a camp teaching you how to-- how to become a shoemaker.
So I-- I transferred to Zurich, and then I applied for a scholarship to go to school.
And then I was free from the camp.
I lived with a-- I had enough-- I think the Swiss Jewish community somehow arranged-- I lived with a Gentile family.
I now forgot their names.
And I went to school, to a-- to a commercial school, in Zurich.
When was this?
That was 1940-- between '40-- around '44, '45.
OK?
And the rest-- the rest of the children set up another [? hashara ?] in [?
Aix-les-Bains, ?] which was the French part of Switzerland.
OK.
They also, they were able to get out of the labor camps-- or the refugee-- I shouldn't call them labor camps.
They were refugee camps, with no-- no hard labor.
Nothing.
I wish we had-- sometimes I wish we had something to do.
There was nothing for us to do.
I learned how to smoke in those camps.
That's about it.
The [INAUDIBLE].
So they-- they set up a [? harshara ?] in [?
Aix-les-Bains. ?] And eventually, when the war was over, all of [?
Aix-les-Bains ?] went to Palestine, OK?
Some of us remained in Switzerland after the war.
I was one of them to remain in Switzerland because I was going to school and-- and everything else.
So--
In Switzerland.
Oh, I got drunk.
[LAUGHS] And a whole bunch of us.
That's the only time I ever got drunk.
You know.
This was-- ?] We're not drinking, we-- we felt ourselves.
You know.
We touched ourselves, you know, am I still-- is it me?
I'm still alive?
The war is over, and I'm still alive.
Oh, Switzerland, we had newspaper, radio, and news, and everything else.
Was great jubilation that the war was-- the war was over.
I was like, thank God.
You know, I mean-- Is it true?
Is-- What-- what happened?
We didn't know-- we didn't know what happened.
The war was over.
I had followed the news, naturally.
Like a-- the news hound I am.
I followed the news, and eventually, the war was over.
So-- but we knew the war in the Pacific was still going on.
So it wasn't all over.
It isn't over till it's over, that's what Yogi Berra once said.
It wasn't over.
But there was great jubilation and everything else.
No, I did not know my mother and brother.
I knew my father was gone.
I had no idea what happened to my mother and brother.
I was not aware of it at all.
And eventually-- eventually, the war was over altogether, I remember.
Even in the Pacific, you know, after the atom bomb in Nagasaki and Hiroshima.
The war was over.
I was still-- I was still in school in Switzerland.
And I was-- naturally, everybody knew where I was, you know, the-- my friends.
So Marko-- Marko who was one of our leaders, he-- he knew where I was.
And actually, we kept in touch.
And he said to me, I want you to come back with me to Italy.
I said, what do you want me to do in Italy?
He said, well, we're gonna-- we're gonna work with the Bricha.
It's part of the Haganah, which I knew it was.
He says, we've got to get some-- we've gotta get some people out of the camps.
Can you explain what is the Bricha?
The Bricha was the-- was a section of the Haganah, the Jewish underground, which was working in different countries trying to help the survivors of the camps.
You see, the concentration camps-- the war was over.
Concentration camps were liberated.
But you had this mass of people that were Jews that nobody wanted.
Nobody wanted Jews.
You know.
They'll-- they'll take Englishmen.
They'll take Dutchmen.
Jews, we don't want.
They didn't-- So there was no place for them to go, except there was one place on earth where they were welcomed.
And that was Palestine, the [?
Shov. ?] OK.
And that's what the Bricha did.
So I said to myself, I owe this.
I owe this to the Jewish people.
I owe this to my-- to my parents, which I didn't know what happened to them.
But here, I had-- I was fluent in Italian.
I knew my way around.
You know.
You could-- I could talk to people.
So I went-- I left school, I went back to Italy, and I went up to one of the most gorgeous parts of Italy.
It's called Merano Bolzano, which is way, way up in the-- in the mountain, the Italian Alps.
As a matter of fact, some of the old people still spoke German there, because in World War I, this belonged to Austria.
In World War-- World War I, this one of the things that was-- was given to Italy.
So the old folks still spoke German.
The new ones only spoke Italian.
So I went up to-- to Merano, and my job was-- [INAUDIBLE] to me.
I had to go up to the mountains.
They had-- they had the Jewish Brigade.
Now, the Jewish brigade was part of the British 5th Army.
When the Jewish Brigade was formed, the British didn't want to give them any-- any nice jobs, so they gave them the trucks.
They became truck drivers, which was a blessing in disguise, because the Jewish Brigade had the transportation.
They had the army trucks, all part of the British army, with the Magen David on the side.
But they were truck drivers.
You know.
It's-- It wasn't right for the British to drive trucks, so they gave it to the Jews.
You know, thank God they did that.
As a matter of fact, we got all the-- all the gasoline for free.
You know, they used to go to-- used to go into the petrol points, which we called the-- the British army calls the-- the gasoline stations, and we used to fill up.
We used to say, King George is paying for all this.
So we used to take them up.
So my job was to go up in the mountains.
On the other side of Austria was another group.
They used to send these-- these people over the mountains to the Italian side.
I used to make sure received them, put them on these-- on these army trucks, which were all manned by-- by Palestinians, Jewish Palestinians.
I'm not using the word Israeli, because there was no Israel.
And then, we used to take them to the first railroad station, and eventually take them by train to Milan, where another group took over.
Now, my job was, first of all, to talk to the Italian police.
So each train has Italian police.
Explain to them all kinds of things.
These are relatives of British military personnel.
You know, I used to give them cigarettes, and-- and we had wine, and everything else.
And you know, I used to tell them we're taking these-- these people over.
We're taking them to Milan.
No problem.
They're no smugglers.
Oh, I would do it-- I would do this trip maybe three times a week.
Three times a week.
And after awhile, I got to know the people.
And they used to-- many times, they used to ask, the Italian police, how come you speak so good Italian?
Yeah, I was dressed as an-- I was dressed as an American officer.
I had all the paraphernalia except the buttons where it says US, you know.
So if American-- American military police would get me, they could not say that I was impersonating an American officer, because certain insignia, I didn't have.
I had some fake decorations.
It was very impressive to the Italians.
They didn't know one thing from the other.
But an American, I would say immediately, this is not an American uniform.
So I used to tell them I was-- I come from-- my parents are Italian.
I was born in Brooklyn, New York.
And that's where I learned Italian.
At home, we spoke Italian.
You know, so that's a-- I have a--
They were very nice.
They really didn't care that much.
You know.
We said, these are not smugglers.
We get them in.
And they said, bring them over.
And sometimes, I had some tough times.
Some of these-- some of these refugees, you know, they were fighting among themselves.
I said, shut, shut, quiet.
This was 5 o'clock in the morning on a train.
Other people are going, and don't-- don't make too much noise.
And I used to get them to Milan.
And in Milan, another group took over.
I usually had about 50.
That's all I could take, 50.
You know, put them on the army trucks. 50 people.
And [INAUDIBLE].
So this was my-- my job with the-- with the Bricha and Haganah.
And this lasted until about 1946.
In 1946, I was [INAUDIBLE].
Things was-- things were already getting organized and everything else.
In '46, '46/'47-- I would say until the end of '46.
I had a cousin who lived in Milan, and he was in the fur business, too.
You know, and says, come-- come work for me.
So I-- I went to Milan.
And I-- I lived in Milan and was with my cousin.
And we were in the fur business.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So I-- that's-- that's about where it ended.
And now I'll-- I'll tell you, you want to know how I came to America.
Would you like to know that?
One of the-- of the liaison officers for the-- for the Haganah was an American colonel.
I think his name was Schwartz.
Don't tell me where he came from.
He used to tip us off where the British military police checkpoints were.
You know.
He was not betraying-- he was not saying anything about the Americans.
He used to tip the Haganah off.
Because he was in American intelligence, he knew where British-- some parts-- some parts was-- was-- was controlled by the British, and some parts by the Americans.
He used to tell us-- we were-- we weren't worried about the Americans.
We were worried about the British.
The British military police checkpoints, he used to tell us.
So I just knew him by-- I used to call him Colonel.
I didn't know Schwartz, where he was, what he was.
I got his name later on.
He told me who he was.
One day, about 1948, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Israel was created, you know, big euphoria, everybody was happy.
I was-- I was happy.
I was-- I was in love with nice Italian girl down there.
You know.
I was a little gigolo in those days, you know, to a certain degree.
I-- you know, I enjoyed life.
I figured I made it.
So one day, I walk on the streets and I see somebody calling me, Arnold.
I turn around.
There is this Colonel Schwartz, who I hadn't seen maybe for a couple of years.
Oh, yeah.
[INAUDIBLE].
He says, well, how are you doing?
Fine.
He says, what are you doing here?
I said, I'm fine.
You know.
I've got a girlfriend.
And I-- I'm making some money.
He says, come on now.
He says, you don't want to hang around in Italy for the rest of your life.
He says-- he says, go to Israel, or go to the United States.
I say, wait a minute.
To Israel, I can go anytime I want to.
All right.
The United States, that sounds a little different.
I said, wait a minute, I have no relatives in the United States, and I knew you needed an affidavit of support.
I knew that from way back.
He says, don't worry about.
Where were you born?
I say, I was born in Germany.
He says, piece of cake.
You want to go to the United States, I'll arrange for you to get to the United States.
But get out of Italy.
You know, a nice Jewish boy, you don't want to be in Italy.
So I said to him, what's the deal?
He explained to me that the United Jewish Appeal went to some rich, millionaire Jews, you know, the New York Jews, the real millionaires.
There's quite a few of them around.
And they go, they says, please sign your name to some affidavits of support.
The-- the United Jewish Appeal, as an organization, could not issue affidavits of support.
I guess they were nonprofit organizations-- or-- someone explained to me what it was.
I'm going back, now, 47 years ago.
But he said, they go to rich Jews, and say, you know, [INAUDIBLE] put your name down there on 10 of these.
These guys are millionaires.
You know.
Don't worry about it.
We'll hand pick the people that come over to the United States.
They will-- We'll take care of it.
So I got-- mine was signed by [INAUDIBLE] Bulova from Bulova watches.
He doesn't know me from beans.
When I came to United States, I sent him a letter thanking him.
My [? people, ?] it never even reached his desk, you know, he signed so many.
But that's how I came to the United States.
I came as an illegal immigrant.
OK, now, there are-- so, I said, OK, it's a deal.
I-- I was willing to go.
I had an uncle living in London who-- who wanted me to come over to visit him in Italy before I went to the United States.
And I had no travel documents.

The only-- the only thing I had was this old, Polish document because in Switzerland, I-- I said I was a Polish citizen and, I think-- I think the Polish government in exile gave me $25 a month, which was a tremendous amount of money, you know, because I was a Polish citizen.
Even though I couldn't speak Polish and everything else.
So I had this Polish-- I had this Polish passport.
And-- So I went to England.
I had to go through Switzerland.
France wouldn't let me in.
France would not let me in with a Polish passport.
So I went to-- I went to England.
On the way back, I got a Swiss visa, a transit visa.
On the way back, I was supposed to-- I was supposed to go back to the Swiss consulate in London and get myself a transit visa back to Italy.
I didn't go.
So when I arrived-- the plane arrives in-- in-- in Switzerland.
You know, right away they-- they question me.
Where is your-- where is your-- your Swiss transit visa?
I knew damn well I didn't have it.
I said, what do you mean?
There wasn't this visa-- No, no, no.
I said, look, you want to check up on me, I used to work for you guys.
I was a military police interpreter.
They checked it out.
They said, OK.
They gave me a visa.
I went back to Italy.
And I came over here on the last-- I came over by boat.
It was called the the steamship It was a Polish owned boat, Polish flag, which-- which went between Genoa, Italy and New York.
So I came over here by boat from Italy.
Sometimes April of-- April of 1949, I think it was.
Something like that.
You know, I don't have the exact date.
April '49.
I first went-- first went to Halifax.
This boat-- this boat went from Geneva-- from-- from Genoa, Italy.
It first landed in Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada.
There-- they-- they let us out, even though we had no Canadian papers.
But, you know, we weren't going anywhere, so four of us, we rented a taxi.
He gave us a tour of Halifax, Nova Scotia.
We landed-- we landed a few days later in New York.
And a friend of mine who is now my close friend, lived in New York.
He met me at the-- at the port.
